part: A Bronx Boyhood in the Cold War
title: Young Communists Calmed by the Depression
summary: Interviewed by David Cline in Louisville in June 2013, Walter Tillow, seventy-three and raised in the Bronx, describes a middle-class childhood with a mother who worked in New York State's unemployment insurance division and a father who ran coin-operated laundromats, parents who had been Young Communist League members in the 1920s before the Depression calmed them down.

title: His Father Took Him to the Rosenberg Funeral
summary: Tillow recalls being thirteen when the Rosenbergs were executed, his parents debating in the kitchen whether his mother should attend the funeral, his father deciding to take him instead, and a split family with radical aunts (one a Communist Party member) on one side and conservatives on the other.

title: A Year for Apartments to Decontrol
summary: He describes Parkchester, a Metropolitan Life development that quietly refused to rent to African Americans and kept apartments vacant a year to decontrol them, the housing movement that chained itself to those doors, and Cold War scenes of his father discarding old newspapers and an aunt unloading the works of Lenin she could not safely keep.

title: A Clipping That Convinced Student Government
summary: Tillow recounts attending Harpur College, a cheap state school full of New York City kids, and how in 1960 as the sit-in movement broke out the students won a sympathy picket of the local Woolworth by producing a newspaper clipping showing Harvard had done the same, overcoming the administration's resistance.

title: Standing in the Quad Against the Take-Cover Drill
summary: He describes moving to the new campus, the statewide one-day shelter drill of the Cold War, and how fifty to seventy students defied the noon take-cover siren by standing in the quad, a dean defending them, after which they organized a Young Socialists group recognized and funded by student government.

title: The Auditorium Was Packed for the Communist Historian
summary: Tillow tells how the president barred a socialist candidate from speaking, the faculty stripped his power to bar speakers, and the next invited name, communist historian Herbert Aptheker, drew seven or eight hundred people despite an American Legion picket threat, then moved to graduate study at the University of Washington and Cornell in economic history, where breakfast gatherings of students formed.

title: The Baltimore Afro-American Led Him South
summary: During a New York newspaper strike, Tillow read clippings from the Baltimore Afro-American posted around Cornell, saw notice of SNCC's 1963 Easter conference at Gammon Theological Seminary in Atlanta, drove down with classmates, met Anne Braden, who steered three of them, including Charlie and Bunny Haney and their infant, to a movement that needed help in Fayette County, Tennessee.

title: A Murder Trial That Started the Movement
summary: He explains Fayette and Haywood County's high Black land ownership, how a murder trial in which prospective jurors swore they had no objection to African Americans voting prompted Korean War veterans to register voters, the white retaliation expelling sharecroppers, the tents set up on Black-owned land, and the Chicago support movement sending food and clothes for the cold winter.

title: Sleeping in a House With No Walls
topic: Forman, Albany Teens, and the March
summary: Tillow recalls that a Chicago packinghouse-workers committee had hired James Forman, later SNCC executive director, to coordinate Fayette County support, how he met Forman in Atlanta that summer, sheltered two or three Albany teenagers Charles Sherrod needed out of South Georgia, stayed in an unfinished house with no walls or plumbing, and after the March on Washington went back to Atlanta to work for SNCC.

title: Courting the Black Vote Privately
summary: He clarifies that the Fayette County project belonged to the Bradens and SCEF, not SNCC, that a white farmer named Redfearn who had run for sheriff could only shelter them briefly because voter registration was dangerous and politically costly, and describes daily registration work, getting jumped once, and taking children swimming in Memphis with donated bathing suits rounded up by friends at Cornell.

title: Black Farmers Who Owned Their Own Land
summary: Tillow describes John McFerren, the movement's leader who ran a gas station and grocery boycotted by whites, a rape accusation against him that the workers helped his family weather, and a draining registration process in Tennessee requiring applicants to read the Constitution before whatever clerk made each session take two or three hours.

title: Like Road Rage on a Country Road
summary: He recounts that only the brave went to register, that ministers who allowed church speaking yielded some recruits, a courthouse-square march, and being waylaid and beaten by a lone man on the road, while noting the worst violence had passed with the tent-city beatings and drive-by shootings, and three newcomers in a ninety-percent-Black county restarted a movement that had slowed.

title: Five Pieces of Paper for One Stamp
summary: Arriving in Atlanta, Tillow worked with Julian Bond in the communications office putting out news releases three times a week, writing outrage letters for John Lewis to quote condemning the Justice Department and FBI, and seizing on stories like the federal government buying post-office land from Byron De La Beckwith while he was tried for murdering Medgar Evers.

title: Getting Bonanza to Cancel
summary: He describes using SNCC's new WATS line to track down performers booked before segregated audiences in Jackson, hotel by hotel, persuading the Bonanza television cast to refuse to appear where Black patrons were seated upstairs, a major publicity coup, and the four-page Student Voice newsletter mailed to several thousand Friends of SNCC off mimeograph stencils.

title: Above a Barber Shop on Raymond Street
summary: Tillow describes the cramped seven-room office above a barber shop, the IBM Selectric typewriters with interchangeable type balls that let them dress up releases, the staff (Bob, Mike Sair, himself, Julian Bond, Dinky Romilly, Forman, the bookkeeper, Ruby Doris Smith Robinson), and how they took the building next door for an AB Dick offset press to do their own printing.

title: A SNCC Record That Never Got Distributed
summary: He recalls the storeroom of unsold SNCC records, the Freedom in the Air album recorded with Pete Seeger that went undistributed after Mercury signed the Freedom Singers (Bertha Gober, Bernice Johnson Reagon, Cordell Reagon, Rutha Mae Harris) to a better deal, the Danny Lyon fundraising posters, and his year of this work until spring 1964, when Ella Baker and Bob Moses asked him to go to Washington for the Mississippi Freedom Democratic Party challenge.

title: Mirroring the Mississippi Democratic Party
summary: Tillow lays out the 1964 summer project's plan to mirror the segregated Mississippi Democratic Party's precinct, county, and state meetings, get turned away, and hold parallel ones to build an integrated delegation for the Atlantic City convention, and recounts Bob Moses outlining it at a Washington fundraiser attended by Joseph Rauh.

title: If You Bring an Integrated Delegation
topic: Rauh, Ella Baker, and Adam Clayton Powell
summary: He recounts Joseph Rauh, UAW general counsel and Humphrey ally from the 1948 convention fight, pledging publicly to get an integrated Mississippi delegation seated, the decision to open a Washington office staffed by Tillow, Frank Smith, and Barbara Jones under Ella Baker, and visiting Adam Clayton Powell Jr., who greeted Baker warmly but, as she predicted, did nothing.

title: A Seedy Strip That Later Burned
summary: Tillow describes opening the office at Fourteenth and U in Washington's African American commercial district, attending the Minnesota, Wisconsin, and Michigan state conventions to win support resolutions, finding help from Congressman Robert Kastenmeier, and being cautioned by Walter Mondale about a graduate student manning his table.

title: Ten Percent for a Minority Report
summary: He explains the strategy worked out in a meeting at Bayard Rustin's office with Forman, Ella Baker, and Frank Smith: securing ten percent of the credentials committee to sign a minority report and ten state delegations to force a roll-call vote, and tracking who sat on the committee, including Edith Green of Oregon, who became a strong supporter after Hamer's testimony.

title: Renting a Hundred Rooms in Atlantic City
summary: Tillow describes pressuring Mississippi parents while the freedom schools and the Schwerner-Goodman-Chaney crisis unfolded, Ella Baker keynoting the state convention, Reggie Robinson finding a hundred-room hotel for the roughly sixty-eight delegates, and the delegation co-chaired by Ed King of Tougaloo and NAACP state president Aaron Henry, with Fannie Lou Hamer, Annie Devine, and Victoria Gray.

title: After the Women Were Crying
summary: He recounts the credentials committee meeting the weekend before a convention with no suspense but the vice-presidential pick, how Fannie Lou Hamer's televised account of being beaten and jailed moved the committee women, and how President Johnson cut away the coverage by calling a hasty press conference about nothing, an early lesson in television's power.

title: They Sent a No-Name Delegation
summary: Tillow describes the regular Mississippians sending a no-name delegation to avoid the attention figures like Eastland, Stennis, or Governor Barnett would draw, that they later worked for Goldwater who carried the state, and how the Freedom Democrats distributed five thousand copies of their legal brief while the regulars, counting on Johnson, said and circulated little.

title: Two Seats in the Balcony
summary: He recalls the boardwalk vigil and delegates lending their badges, the regular Mississippians going home to empty seats, and the final offer that the Freedom Democrats receive only two at-large observer seats in the balcony, with the convention naming Ed King and Aaron Henry, while Humphrey and Reuther twisted arms and spread word the compromise had been accepted.

title: Luminaries Came to the Church
summary: Tillow describes the morning church meeting where Bayard Rustin, King, Jack Pratt, James Farmer, and Wayne Morse pressed the delegation to accept the compromise, Morse warning that forcing a walkout would help Goldwater, and young SNCC people getting their first taste of Democratic Party politics as the steam went out of the wavering delegates.

title: Evans and Novak Started Red-Baiting
topic: From Atlantic City to the House
summary: He recounts the disillusionment that hardened after volunteers watched the compromise forced down their throats, the syndicated Evans and Novak column red-baiting SNCC, and how the episode led into the 1965 challenge that forced three Mississippi Delta congressmen including Jamie Whitten to stand aside, work taken up by lawyers Kunstler and Kinoy and figures like John Conyers and Elizabeth Holtzman.

title: More Disillusioning Than Energizing
summary: Tillow reflects that Atlantic City was more disillusioning than energizing, seeding bickering and the beginnings of black power as attention turned to Alabama and Stokely Carmichael in Lowndes County, and recounts being in King's hotel room amid the arm-twisting, where Rauh told the delegation he could not be with them if no one else was.

title: Only Castenmeier and Green Stood
summary: He describes the pressure put on credentials-committee members and delegates to accept the compromise, a California judgeship and other favors dangled to make people fall in line, only Kastenmeier and Edith Green holding firm, and the sense that the insiders who knew Reuther and Johnson understood the deal better than the movement did.

title: Some Illusions Were Stripped Away
summary: Tillow gives his reading that the Freedom Democrats came up against the Democratic Party and had illusions stripped away, that the rank-and-file delegates who were freedom fighters in their own communities saw through the two-seat compromise and refused it, and that, with no closing meeting, everyone moved on to the next thing, Lawrence Guyot and others continuing the Mississippi work.

title: A Letter to Found a White Student Group
summary: He recounts going back to encourage the labor movement into civil rights and southern organizing, running workshops at Highlander with leftist union figures like Jesse Prosten of the packinghouse workers, and helping Carl Braden and Jim Williams found a white southern student group that became SOC, building on Bob Zellner, Jane Stembridge, and Sam Shirah's campus work.

title: They Met at Highlander
topic: Going to Work for the UE
summary: Tillow describes leaving for the United Electrical Workers in late 1965, meeting his future wife Kay Marlowe at a Highlander workshop, her path through the University of Illinois NAACP chapter and freedom-vote work to organizing a wildcat miners' movement in eastern Kentucky led by Berman Gibson, where men were charged with conspiring to blow up a bridge still standing.

title: The Police Rounded Up Everyone With Big Ears
summary: He recounts being sent to Lynchburg, Virginia to organize a General Electric plant, observing a rape trial with attorneys William Kunstler and Philip Hirschkop in which the police arrested every man with big ears on the victim's sole description, a youngster with a shaky alibi was convicted then later cleared, and the local paper was required to brand Kunstler a radical New York lawyer in every story.

title: Twenty Years in Pittsburgh
topic: Anti-War Work and the Hospital Union
summary: Tillow describes losing the Lynchburg election, marrying Kay, and moving to Pittsburgh for about twenty years of organizing, involvement in the anti-Vietnam-War movement and the 1965 Washington peace demonstrations, and joining the staff of the 1199 hospital workers union as it weighed expanding from Johns Hopkins to Charleston.

title: Living on One Income
topic: The Communist Party and the Print Shop
summary: He recounts that Pennsylvania's 1969 public-employee bargaining law let Kay organize community hospitals and nursing homes, that he joined the Communist Party and worked largely unpaid after the 1972 McGovern campaign, ran the print shop that produced the People's Weekly World in Camden from 1990 to 1995, and was fired from jobs at the Amalgamated and US Steel once his party membership was discovered.

title: The Dollar Bill With Gregory's Picture
summary: Tillow lists later efforts: trying to run a King-Spock ticket in 1968 and getting Dick Gregory and Mark Lane on Pennsylvania's Peace and Freedom Party ballot, John Conyers's 1978 shorter-work-week bill, anti-Reagan work, and solidarity campaigns for Cuba and for Chile after the 1973 coup against Allende, including helping a Chilean machinist resettle in Pittsburgh.

title: Closest to Her Mother
summary: He describes Kay leaving Pittsburgh in 1990 to organize nurses in Paducah and at Audubon Hospital in Louisville while they commuted for fifteen years, his retirement and move in 2005, and settling in Louisville, Kay's home state and Braden territory, to be near her mother, who lived to about a hundred and one.

title: Illusions Stripped Away, and Recreated
summary: Tillow offers his interpretation that Atlantic City stripped away illusions about the Democratic Party that have since been recreated, the absurdity in retrospect of an issue the party would not concede, and points to the Eisenhower-appointed Fifth Circuit judges, Minor Wisdom, Elbert Tuttle, and Frank Johnson, as the titans who saved the system judicially against an Eastland-and-Stennis-approved bench.

title: They Just Weren't Compromisers
summary: He closes that without the Fifth Circuit judges, aggressive Guild lawyers like Ben Smith and Bruce Waltzer and Len Holt, and the principled non-compromisers of the left such as the Bradens, Virginia Durr, and Ella Baker, the movement would have been in deep trouble, that the chance to beat the Dixiecrats through the Black vote went unused because Reuther and Humphrey had bigger fish to fry, and the interview ends with mutual thanks.
